serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried spicy glass noodles with vegetables is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at a Thai casual dining restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pad Woon Sen (Stir-fried Glass Noodles) — estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central glass noodle and stir-fried vegetable dish components
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Stir-fried Spicy Tang Hoon with Vegetables' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated by combining FAO/INFOODS SEA data for cooked glass noodles (mung bean starch noodles) with USDA SR Legacy data for stir-fried mixed vegetables, chilli, garlic, and typical Thai stir-fry sauce components (soy sauce, oyster sauce, oil). The dish is predominantly glass noodles (~55% by weight cooked) with vegetables (~30%) and sauce/oil (~15%). Energy and macros are weighted accordingly. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glass noodles (mung bean starch noodles, cooked) — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Stir-fried Spicy Tang Hoon with Vegetables'. Glass noodles (cellophane noodles / wun sen) made from mung bean starch have a reported GI of approximately 39–45 (low GI) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The stir-fry preparation with vegetables and sauce does not substantially raise GI. A GI of 45 is used as a conservative upper estimate for the composite dish. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 58.5) / 100 = 26.3 (serving size 300 g, carbohydrate 19.5 g/100g × 3 = 58.5 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from glass noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although glass noodles have a low GI (~45), the glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 26 g, which is in the high GL range; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a plate and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and moderate fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1260 mg per serving) and moderate fat from stir-fry oil pose a concern for cardiovascular disease management; choosing a reduced-oil, reduced-sauce preparation and limiting serving frequency is recommended for individuals with established CVD.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Spicy Glass Noodles with Vegetables (known in Singapore as Tang Hoon stir-fry and in Thai as Pad Wun Sen Phet or วุ้นเส้นผัดเผ็ดกับผัก) is a Thai stir-fried noodle dish made from glass noodles (cellophane noodles / wun sen) derived from mung bean starch, stir-fried at high heat with mixed vegetables such as cabbage, carrot, spring onion, and bean sprouts, seasoned with chilli, garlic, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, and finished with a drizzle of vegetable oil. The dish has a translucent, slightly chewy noodle texture with a spicy, savoury, and mildly sweet flavour profile from the sauce combination. It is a popular item at Thai casual dining restaurants in Singapore, including Sanook Kitchen. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~118 kcal per 100 g) with a low glycemic index (~45) due to the mung bean starch base, but carries a significant sodium load (~420 mg/100 g) from the sauce components. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for those avoiding dairy, but contains soy and potential gluten from oyster sauce. Clinically, the moderate glycemic load and high sodium content are relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The vegetable content contributes dietary fibre (~1.4 g/100 g), vitamin A, and vitamin C.
common_user_queries: Is Pad Wun Sen low GI?
Is tang hoon suitable for weight loss?
What are glass noodles made from?

glass noodles: vermicelli de fécule de haricot mungo

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA glass noodle data and USDA SR Legacy stir-fried vegetable and sauce component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mung bean glass noodles. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this composite dish. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the plant-oil stir-fry preparation method with no hydrogenated fats used. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 g per 100 g; this is naturally zero as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients in its standard vegetable-only preparation. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg per 100 g; this is naturally zero as the standard preparation uses no animal products. The food_name has been normalised from the field survey menu label '615D Stir-fried Spicy Tang Hoon with Veg' to the canonical English name 'Stir-fried Spicy Glass Noodles with Vegetables'; the original menu label is preserved in aliases. The cuisine tag 'Pad Thai' in the input is not applicable to this dish and has been disregarded; this dish is a glass noodle stir-fry, not a Pad Thai preparation. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from typical vegetable oil composition used in Thai stir-frying.
